author: @colinatkinson9353
text: Rob please remember that not all electric motors can act as a generator.  A single phase induction motor for example can not act as a generator or alternator.  Permanent magnet motors on the other hand can act as a generator.

replies.author: @Teknopottu
replies.text: Rob has a video about this. You can generate with induction motors when using connected capacitors and the residual magnetism of the rotor.

author: @charlesbova722
text: Good morning, with that in mind is it possible to use the front wheels as a generator and charge a separate battery than the drive wheel( 2 batteries one charging and 1 driving) ? your awesome thank you b well and stay strong 💪

replies.author: @AnalystPrime
replies.text: Technically yes, but there is no point. Simply connecting battery A to battery B through a converter so the 400V battery pack does not fry the 12V battery charges B at some 80-90% efficiency. Using the EV battery pack to turn the engines which turn the wheels which moves the car which turns the other wheels which move the generators which send electricity through a converter which is again needed so it does not fry the 12V battery would be maybe 40-50% efficient and also the extra drag from your front wheels basically braking the whole time means your range would drop like crazy.

author: @domestinger8805
text: One thing never discussed is acceleration/deceleration and how the waveform changes between switch on > power up > switch off > power down. Every motor/generator has an unique waveform associated with its ergonomical characteristics; it requires amps to initiate acceleration and provide torque, voltage for speed and capacity. This means that we ideally wish to give the motor, or harvest from the generator, an optimum electrical wave form of a particular shape in time, varying between amps and volts. In theory, this could also mean, provided we had good bearings, we could make a flywheel of a large mass, start it up by hand and feed it very low amps and high voltage to keep it spinning, then harvest from it however plausible.

author: @ToninFightsEntropy
text:
Cheers Rob! That is very helpful! Until now I thought there was a different construction that would be more efficient for one than the other, like with speakers/microphones!
Now I know to measure my motors when I try to make a generator. I have a digital tachometer to measure motor speeds, which works great. Just need to put the right voltage in to test their rpm. I don't think they're always quite what they say on the box btw! :P Atleast that's been my experience with a lot of components so far with my messing around, motors often off by about 20%, not seen one more accurate than to 5%. Hell, potentiometers from Jaycar seem to be off by up to 500%, which is just hilariously unusable to a noob like me haha.
I think eventually  though I'd like to make a water battery system with multiple pumps to handle excess power, and hook in solar, wind, temperature difference, and manual power inputs to something that just engages more storage with the excess, and drains them back down for power through a generator that requires a specific flow rate to turn the gears to squeeze as much as possible out of it.
Assuming I can store a comparable amount of energy that way compared to feasible alternatives.. I really want to mix a lot of technologies together in the simplest most efficient ways possible, e.g. and your videos are filling my head with ideas and arming me with the information I need to have more of a go and discuss ideas, so I'd love to hear what you think of any of that! :)

author: @VacuumTube88
text: What some people seem confused about often reading comments is really the efficiency of the motor in converting motion into electricity, people basically want to know which electrical motor, arrangend as a generator is the most efficient in converting mechanical energy into electricity, and that is partially meaningless, because the answer should be alternators (especially the brushless ones) but that efficiency depends a lot on controlled speed, a car alternator is about 70-80% efficient but a power station alternator can reach 98%, they are almost identical beside the size, but the the controlled speed at which those are operated is the key factor there.

replies.author: @clivelee4279
replies.text: Which automotive alternator, has efficiencies in the 70-80% range please ,my understanding is that you’re lucky to get 50% . Thanks.

author: @Bigman-hi2ne
text: Out of curiosity is that 150 watts a second, or is that it’s wattage over an hour? Or neither? Thank you for your videos they are extremely helpful.

replies.author: @brianmurphy8790
replies.text:
150 watts over 1 hour is 0.15 kwh.
150 watts over 10 hours is 1.5 kwh.
If an appliance is rated at 100w, it'll take 10 hours to consume 1 kw of power.
If an appliance is rated at 1000w, it'll take 1 hour to consume 1 kw of power.
